topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a casual ‘seen’ from Alex. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I scrolled through my feed, a deliberate, shaky movement, trying to appear unbothered as I reread the last few exchanges. Alex had been so open, so enthusiastic about their 'city adventures,' painting vivid pictures of exploring new neighborhoods. Now, the reality hit with the force of a physical blow. I looked out my window, the familiar skyline suddenly feeling suffocatingly small. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable. I quickly minimized the chat window, my fingers trembling slightly as I reached for my phone, wanting nothing more than to disappear into the quiet of my apartment.

[story 2]

It started subtly—a shared mention of a specific coffee shop, a local park bench. Then came the undeniable proof: a geo-tagged photo of a landmark we’d both vaguely discussed, taken from the exact same vantage point. I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat. The air in the room seemed to thicken, making it hard to draw a full breath. Every casual interaction we’d shared, every inside joke, suddenly recontextualized itself, layered with a shared, tangible geography. I started pacing the length of my living room, my movements jerky and agitated. When I finally spoke to myself, the sound was thin, reedy, betraying the sudden, sharp sting of exposure.

[story 3]

I kept refreshing the messaging app, a frantic, pointless ritual. The silence from Alex was deafening, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my temples. I glanced at my reflection in the darkened monitor—my face was pale, my eyes wide, betraying a profound sense of being caught in a snare. It wasn't just that they lived nearby; it was the implication, the shared, invisible space where our digital camaraderie had suddenly collided with physical proximity. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. I took a deep, ragged inhale, trying to steady the tremor in my hands, but the effort felt exhausting, leaving me feeling utterly exposed under the weight of the realization.
